subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: What would be the IUPAC name and symbol for the element with atomic number 120?
question_html: What would be the IUPAC name and symbol for the element with atomic number 120?
solution_text: The roots for 1, 2 and 0 are un, bi, and nil, respectively. Hence, the symbol and the name respectively are Ubn and unbinilium.
solution_html: The roots for 1, 2 and 0 are un, bi, and nil, respectively. Hence, the symbol and the name respectively are Ubn and unbinilium.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: How would you justify the presence of 18 elements in the 5th period of the Periodic Table?
question_html: How would you justify the presence of 18 elements in the 5th period of the Periodic Table?
solution_text: When n = 5 (5 th period), l = 0, 1, 2, 3. The order in which the energy of the available orbitals 4d, 5s, and 5p increases are 5s < 4d < 5p. The total number of orbitals available is 9. The maximum number of electrons that can be accommodated is 18; and therefore 18 elements are there in the 5th period.
solution_html: When n = 5 (5th period), l = 0, 1, 2, 3. The order in which the energy of the available orbitals 4d, 5s, and 5p increases are 5s < 4d < 5p. The total number of orbitals available is 9. The maximum number of electrons that can be accommodated is 18; and therefore 18 elements are there in the 5th period.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: The elements Z = 117 and 120 have not yet been discovered. In which family/group would you place these elements and also give the electronic configuration in each case.
question_html: The elements Z = 117 and 120 have not yet been discovered. In which family/group would you place these elements and also give the electronic configuration in each case.
solution_text: From the periodic table, that element with Z = 117, would belong to the halogen family (Group 17) and the electronic configuration would be [Rn] 5f 14 6d 10 7s 2 7p 5 . The element with Z = 120, will be placed in Group 2 (alkaline earth metals), and will have the electronic configuration [Uuo]8s 2 .
solution_html: From the periodic table, that element with Z = 117, would belong to the halogen family (Group 17) and the electronic configuration would be [Rn] 5f146d107s27p5. The element with Z = 120, will be placed in Group 2 (alkaline earth metals), and will have the electronic configuration [Uuo]8s2.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Considering the atomic number and position in the periodic table, arrange the following elements in the increasing order of metallic character: Si, Be, Mg, Na, P.
question_html: Considering the atomic number and position in the periodic table, arrange the following elements in the increasing order of metallic character: Si, Be, Mg, Na, P.
solution_text: As we know the metallic character decreases as we move left to right (along the period) in the periodic table and increases as we move top to bottom (in group). Therefore, correct order of increase in metallic character is: P < Si < Be < Mg < Na
solution_html:
As we know the metallic character decreases as we move left to right (along the period) in the periodic table and  increases as we move top to bottom (in group). Therefore, correct order of increase in metallic character is:
P < Si < Be < Mg < Na

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Which of the following species will have the largest and the smallest size? Mg, Mg 2+ , Al, Al 3+ .
question_html: Which of the following species will have the largest and the smallest size? Mg, Mg2+, Al, Al3+.
solution_text: Atomic radii decrease across a period. Cations are smaller than their parent atoms. Among isoelectronic species, the one with the larger positive nuclear charge will have a smaller radius. Hence the largest species is Mg; the smallest one is Al 3+ .
solution_html:
Atomic radii decrease across a period. Cations are smaller than their parent atoms. Among isoelectronic species, the one with the larger positive nuclear charge will have a smaller radius.
Hence the largest species is Mg; the smallest one is Al3+.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: The first ionization enthalpy ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} i H ) values of the third-period elements, Na, Mg, and Si are respectively 496, 737 and 786 kJ mol –1 . Predict whether the first {tex}\Delta{/tex} i H value for Al will be more close to 575 or 760 kJ mol –1 ? Justify your answer.
question_html: The first ionization enthalpy ({tex}\Delta{/tex}i H ) values of the third-period elements, Na, Mg, and Si are respectively 496, 737 and 786 kJ mol–1. Predict whether the first {tex}\Delta{/tex}i H value for Al will be more close to 575 or 760 kJ mol–1? Justify your answer.
solution_text: It will be more close to 575 kJ mol –1 . The value for Al should be lower than that of Mg because of the effective shielding of 3p electrons from the nucleus by 3s - electrons.
solution_html: It will be more close to 575 kJ mol–1. The value for Al should be lower than that of Mg because of the effective shielding of 3p electrons from the nucleus by 3s-electrons.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Which of the following will have the most negative electron gain enthalpy and which the least negative? P, S, Cl, F. Explain your answer.
question_html: Which of the following will have the most negative electron gain enthalpy and which the least negative? P, S, Cl, F. Explain your answer.
solution_text: Electron gain enthalpy generally becomes more negative across a period as we move from left to right. Within a group, electron gain enthalpy becomes less negative down a group. However, adding an electron to the 2p-orbital leads to greater repulsion than adding an electron to the larger 3p-orbital. Hence the element with most negative electron gain enthalpy is chlorine; the one with the least negative electron gain enthalpy is phosphorus.
solution_html: Electron gain enthalpy generally becomes more negative across a period as we move from left to right. Within a group, electron gain enthalpy becomes less negative down a group. However, adding an electron to the 2p-orbital leads to greater repulsion than adding an electron to the larger 3p-orbital. Hence the element with most negative electron gain enthalpy is chlorine; the one with the least negative electron gain enthalpy is phosphorus.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Using the Periodic Table, predict the formulas of compounds that might be formed by the following pairs of elements; silicon and bromine aluminium and sulphur.
question_html:
Using the Periodic Table, predict the formulas of compounds that might be formed by the following pairs of elements;

  1. silicon and bromine
  2. aluminium and sulphur.
solution_text: Silicon is a group 14 element with a valence of 4; bromine belongs to the halogen family with a valence of 1. Hence the formula of the compound formed would be SiBr 4 . Aluminium belongs to group 13 with a valence of 3; sulphur belongs to group 16 elements with a valence of 2. Hence, the formula of the compound formed would be Al 2 S 3 .
solution_html:
1. Silicon is a group 14 element with a valence of 4; bromine belongs to the halogen family with a valence of 1. Hence the formula of the compound formed would be SiBr4.
  2. Aluminium belongs to group 13 with a valence of 3; sulphur belongs to group 16 elements with a valence of 2. Hence, the formula of the compound formed would be Al2S3.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Are the oxidation state and covalency of Al in [AlCl(H 2 O) 5 ] 2+ the same?
question_html: Are the oxidation state and covalency of Al in [AlCl(H2O)5]2+ the same?
solution_text: No. The oxidation state of Al is +3 and the covalency is 6.
solution_html: No. The oxidation state of Al is +3 and the covalency is 6.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Show by a chemical reaction with water that Na 2 O is a basic oxide and Cl 2 O 7 , is an acidic oxide.
question_html: Show by a chemical reaction with water that Na2O is a basic oxide and Cl2O7, is an acidic oxide.
solution_text: Na 2 O reacts with water to form sodium oxide which turns red litmus blue. {tex}{N{a_2}O + {H_2}O} \to {2NaOH}{/tex} Therefore, Na 2 O is a basic oxides. In contrast, Cl 2 O 7 reacts with water to form perchloric acid which turns blue litmus red. {tex}C{l_2}{O_7} + {H_2}O \to {2HCl{O_4}}{/tex} Therefore, Cl 2 O 7 is an acidic oxide.
solution_html:
Na2O reacts with water to form sodium oxide which turns red litmus blue.
{tex}{N{a_2}O + {H_2}O} \to {2NaOH}{/tex}
Therefore, Na2O is a basic oxides.
In contrast, Cl2O7 reacts with water to form perchloric acid which turns blue litmus red.
{tex}C{l_2}{O_7} + {H_2}O \to {2HCl{O_4}}{/tex}
Therefore, Cl2O7 is an acidic oxide.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: What is the basic theme of organisation in the periodic table?
question_html: What is the basic theme of organisation in the periodic table?
solution_text: The basic theme of organisation of elements in the periodic table is to classify the elements in periods and groups according to their properties. This systematic arrangement makes the study of elements and their compounds, simple to understand and easy to interpert.
solution_html: The basic theme of organisation of elements in the periodic table is to classify the elements in periods and groups according to their properties. This systematic arrangement makes the study of elements and their compounds, simple to understand and easy to interpert.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Which important property did Mendeleev use to classify the elements in this periodic table and did he stick to that?
question_html: Which important property did Mendeleev use to classify the elements in this periodic table and did he stick to that?
solution_text: In his period table, Mendeleev used the periodicity in atomic weight as the basis of classification of elements. He did stick with the idea of atomic weight, and used it to classify elements into groups and periods. He went further when based on his research he corrected atomic weights of exisiting elements and even kept spaces of elements that he felt should exit but not found at that time such as gallium, germanium etc.
solution_html: In his period table, Mendeleev used the periodicity in atomic weight as the basis of classification of elements. He did stick with the idea of atomic weight, and used it to classify elements into groups and periods. He went further when based on his research he corrected atomic weights of exisiting elements and even kept spaces of elements that he felt should exit but not found at that time such as gallium, germanium etc.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: What is the basic difference in approach between Mendeleev's periodic law and the Modern Periodic Law?
question_html: What is the basic difference in approach between Mendeleev's periodic law and the Modern Periodic Law?
solution_text: In Mendeleev's periodic law, physical and chemical properties of elements are periodic functions of their atomic weights whereas the Modern Periodic Law uses atomic numbers as the measure of the periodicity.
solution_html: In Mendeleev's periodic law, physical and chemical properties of elements are periodic functions of their atomic weights whereas the Modern Periodic Law uses atomic numbers as the measure of the periodicity.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: On the basis of quantum numbers, justify that the sixth period of the periodic table should have 32 elements.
question_html: On the basis of quantum numbers, justify that the sixth period of the periodic table should have 32 elements.
solution_text: The sixth period corresponds to sixth shell. The orbitals present in this shell are 6s, 4f, 5p, and 6d. The maximum number of electrons which can be present in these sub-shell is 2 + 14 + 6 + 10 = 32. Since the number of elements in a period corresponds to the number of electrons in the shells, therefore, sixth period should have a maximum of 32 elements.
solution_html: The sixth period corresponds to sixth shell. The orbitals present in this shell are 6s, 4f, 5p, and 6d. The maximum number of electrons which can be present in these sub-shell is 2 + 14 + 6 + 10 = 32. Since the number of elements in a period corresponds to the number of electrons in the shells, therefore, sixth period should have a maximum of 32 elements.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: In terms of period and group where will you locate the element with Z = 114?
question_html: In terms of period and group where will you locate the element with Z = 114?
solution_text: For Z = 114, the electronic configuration is [Rn] 5f 14 6d 10 7s 2 7p 2 . The outermost electronic configuration provides the information that the element lies in the 7 th period and belongs to the carbon family i.e. Group 14.
solution_html: For Z = 114, the electronic configuration is  [Rn] 5f14 6d10 7s2 7p2. The outermost electronic configuration provides the information that the element lies in the 7th period and belongs to the carbon family i.e. Group 14.

solution_text: The element is chlorine (Cl) with atomic number (Z) = 17.
solution_html: The element is chlorine (Cl) with atomic number (Z) = 17.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Why do elements in the same group have similar physical and chemical properties?
question_html: Why do elements in the same group have similar physical and chemical properties?
solution_text: Elements in the same group in the periodic table have similar chemical properties because their atoms have the same number of electrons in the highest occupied energy level. In other words, all the elements in the group has the same outermost electronic configuration.
solution_html: Elements in the same group in the periodic table have similar chemical properties because their atoms have the same number of electrons in the highest occupied energy level. In other words, all the elements in the group has the same outermost electronic configuration.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: What does atomic radius and ionic radius really mean to you?
question_html: What does atomic radius and ionic radius really mean to you?
solution_text: Atomic radius: The distance from the centre of nucleus to the outermost shell of electrons in the atom of any element is called its atomic radius. It refers to both covalent or metallic radius depending on whether the element is a non-metal or a metal. Ionic radius: The ionic radii is defined as the internuclear distance between the two ions in an ionic crystal.
solution_html:
Atomic radius: The distance from the centre of nucleus to the outermost shell of electrons in the atom of any element is called its atomic radius. It refers to both covalent or metallic radius depending on whether the element is a non-metal or a metal.
Ionic radius: The ionic radii is defined as the internuclear distance between the two ions in an ionic crystal.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: How do atomic radius vary in a period and in a group? How do you explain the variation?
question_html: How do atomic radius vary in a period and in a group? How do you explain the variation?
solution_text: Atomic radius increases down the group and decreases across the period. This is due to the continuous increase in the number of electronic shells or orbit numbers in the structure of atoms of the elements down the group. From left to right across a period atomic radii generally decrease due to an increase in effective nuclear charge.
solution_html:
Atomic radius increases down the group and decreases across the period. This is due to the continuous increase in the number of electronic shells or orbit numbers in the structure of atoms of the elements down the group.
From left to right across a period atomic radii generally decrease due to an increase in effective nuclear charge.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: What do you understand by isoelectronic species? Name a species that will be isoelectronic with each of the following atoms or ions. F – Ar Mg 2+ Rb +
question_html:
What do you understand by isoelectronic species? Name a species that will be isoelectronic with each of the following atoms or ions. 

  1. F– 
  2. Ar
  3. Mg2+ 
  4. Rb+
solution_text: Iso-electronic species are atoms or ions having the same number of electrons but different magnitudes of nuclear charge. F - ion has 9+1 = 10 electrons. The isoelectronic species of Fluoride will also have 10 electrons. For example, Na + ion [11-1 = 10 electrons], Ne [10 electrons], O 2- ion [8+2 =10 electrons], and Al 3+ ion [13-3 = 10 electrons]. Ar has 18 electrons. The isoelectronic species of Argon will also have 18 electrons. For example, S 2- ion [16+2 = 18 electrons], Cl - ion [17+1 = 18 electrons], K + ion [19-1 = 18 electrons], and Ca + ion [20-2 = 18 electrons]. Mg 2+ ion has 12 – 2 = 10 electrons. The isoelectronic species of Magnesium will also have 10 electrons. For example, F - ion [9+1 = 10 electrons], Ne [10 electrons’, O 2- ion [8+2 = 10 electrons], and Al 3+ ion [13-3 = 10 electrons]. Rb + ion has 37 – 1 = 36 electrons. The isoelectronic species of Rubidium will also have 36 electrons. For example, Br - ion [35+1 = 36 electrons], Kr [36 electrons], and Sr 2+ ion [38-2 = 36 electrons].
solution_html:
Iso-electronic species are atoms or ions having the same number of electrons but different magnitudes of nuclear charge.

  1. F- ion has 9+1 = 10 electrons. The isoelectronic species of Fluoride will also have 10 electrons. For example, Na+ ion [11-1 = 10 electrons], Ne [10 electrons], O2- ion [8+2 =10 electrons], and Al3+ ion [13-3 = 10 electrons].
  2. Ar has 18 electrons. The isoelectronic species of Argon will also have 18 electrons. For example, S2- ion [16+2 = 18 electrons], Cl- ion [17+1 = 18 electrons], K+ ion [19-1 = 18 electrons], and Ca+ ion [20-2 = 18 electrons].
  3. Mg2+ ion has 12 – 2 = 10 electrons. The isoelectronic species of Magnesium will also have 10 electrons. For example, F- ion [9+1 = 10 electrons], Ne [10 electrons’, O2- ion [8+2 = 10 electrons], and Al3+ ion [13-3 = 10 electrons].
  4. Rb+ ion has 37 – 1 = 36 electrons. The isoelectronic species of Rubidium will also have 36 electrons. For example, Br- ion [35+1 = 36 electrons], Kr [36 electrons], and Sr2+ ion [38-2 = 36 electrons].

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Consider the following species: N 3- , O 2- , F - , Na + , Mg 2+ and Al 3+ What is common in them? Arrange them in order of increasing radii.
question_html:
Consider the following species:
N3-, O2-, F-, Na+, Mg2+ and Al3+

  1. What is common in them?
  2. Arrange them in order of increasing radii.
solution_text: They all are iso-electronic with ten electrons each. In iso-electronic species, greater the nuclear charge, lesser will be the atomic or ionic radius. Al 3+ < Mg 2+ ,Na + <F - < O 2- <N 3-
solution_html:
1. They all are iso-electronic with ten electrons each.
  2. In iso-electronic species, greater the nuclear charge, lesser will be the atomic or ionic radius.
    Al3+ < Mg2+ ,Na+ <F- < O2- <N3-

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Explain why cation are smaller and anions larger in radii than their parent atoms?
question_html: Explain why cation are smaller and anions larger in radii than their parent atoms?
solution_text: A variation in size of a cation or anion is due the difference in effective nuclear charge on the outermost electrons. In cation, on loss of an electron the effective nuclear charge experienced is more by the remaining valence electrons. They are more tightly held towards centre, thereby the size of the cation decreases. On the other hand in anion, the effective nuclear charge on valence electrons decrease with addition of each new electron. Moreover, there are more inter-electron repulsions in the valence shell. The combination of these two effects eventually increase the size of anion.
solution_html: A variation in size of a cation or anion is due the difference in effective nuclear charge on the outermost electrons. In cation, on loss of an electron the effective nuclear charge experienced is more by the remaining valence electrons. They are more tightly held towards centre, thereby the size of the cation decreases. On the other hand in anion, the effective nuclear charge on valence electrons decrease with addition of each new electron. Moreover, there are more inter-electron repulsions in the valence shell. The combination of these two effects eventually increase the size of anion.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: What is the significance of the terms - isolated gaseous atom and ground state while defining the ionization enthalpy and electron gain enthalpy?
question_html: What is the significance of the terms - isolated gaseous atom and ground state while defining the ionization enthalpy and electron gain enthalpy?
solution_text: Significance of term 'isolated gaseous atom'. The atoms in the gaseous state are for separated in the sense that they do not have any mutual attractive and repulsive interactions. These are therefore regarded as isolated atoms. In this state the value of ionization enthalpy and electron gain enthalpy are not influenced by the presence of the other atoms. It is not possible to express these when the atoms are in the liquid or solid state due to the presence of inter atomic forces. Significance of ground state. Ground state of the atom represents the normal energy state of an atom. It means electrons in a particular atom are in the lowest energy state and they neither lose nor gain electron. Both ionization enthalpy and electron gain enthalpy are generally expressed with respect to the ground state of an atom only.
solution_html:
1. Significance of term 'isolated gaseous atom'. The atoms in the gaseous state are for separated in the sense that they do not have any mutual attractive and repulsive interactions. These are therefore regarded as isolated atoms. In this state the value of ionization enthalpy and electron gain enthalpy are not influenced by the presence of the other atoms. It is not possible to express these when the atoms are in the liquid or solid state due to the presence of inter atomic forces.
  2. Significance of ground state. Ground state of the atom represents the normal energy state of an atom. It means electrons in a particular atom are in the lowest energy state and they neither lose nor gain electron. Both ionization enthalpy and electron gain enthalpy are generally expressed with respect to the ground state of an atom only.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Energy of an electron in the ground state of the hydrogen atom is {tex} - 2.18 \times {10^{ - 18}}J{/tex} . Calculate the ionization enthalpy of atomic hydrogen in terms of J mol -1 .
question_html: Energy of an electron in the ground state of the hydrogen atom is {tex} - 2.18 \times {10^{ - 18}}J{/tex}. Calculate the ionization enthalpy of atomic hydrogen in terms of J mol-1.
solution_text: The ionisation enthalpy is for 1 mole atoms. Therefore, ground state energy of the atoms may be expressed as E (ground state) = {tex}( - 2.18 \times {10^{ - 18}}J) \times (6.022 \times {10^{23}}mo{l^{ - 1}}){/tex} {tex} = - 1.312 \times {10^6}J\;mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} Ionisation enthalpy {tex} = E\infty - E{/tex} ground state {tex} = 0 - ( - 1.312 \times {10^6}J\;mo{l^{ - 1}}){/tex} {tex} = 1.312 \times {10^6}J\;mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
The ionisation enthalpy is for 1 mole atoms. Therefore, ground state energy of the atoms may be expressed as
E (ground state) = {tex}( - 2.18 \times {10^{ - 18}}J) \times (6.022 \times {10^{23}}mo{l^{ - 1}}){/tex}
{tex} = - 1.312 \times {10^6}J\;mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Ionisation enthalpy {tex} = E\infty - E{/tex} ground state
{tex} = 0 - ( - 1.312 \times {10^6}J\;mo{l^{ - 1}}){/tex}
{tex} = 1.312 \times {10^6}J\;mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Among the second period elements, the actual ionization enthalpies are in the order: Li < B < Be < C < O < N < F < Ne Explain why Be has higher {tex}\Delta{/tex} i H than B? O has lower {tex}\Delta{/tex} i H than N and F?
question_html:
Among the second period elements, the actual ionization enthalpies are in the order:
Li < B < Be < C < O < N < F < Ne
Explain why

  1. Be has higher {tex}\Delta{/tex}i H than B?
  2. O has lower {tex}\Delta{/tex}i H than N and F?
solution_text: Both the observation can be explained in terms of extra-stability of fully filled or half-filled orbitals. The electronic configuration of Be is 1s 2 2s 2 ; whereas that of B is 1s 2 2s 2 2p 1 . Be has a completely filled valence orbital and will not easily loose electrons from them to form a cation. B, on the other hand, will easily loose the lone p electron to acquire B like stable configuration. N has the electron configuration 1s 2 2s 2 2p 3 , where in the outermost shell s is completely and p is half filled. It is a stable configuration as compared to oxygen whose electronic configuration is 1s 2 2s 2 2p 4 . Oxygen will rapidly loose its extra p-electron to acquire a N-like stable configuration.
solution_html:
Both the observation can be explained in terms of extra-stability of fully filled or half-filled orbitals.

  1. The electronic configuration of Be is 1s2 2s2; whereas that of B is 1s2 2s2 2p1. Be has a completely filled valence orbital and will not easily loose electrons from them to form a cation. B, on the other hand, will easily loose the lone p electron to acquire B like stable configuration.
  2. N has the electron configuration 1s2 2s2 2p3, where in the outermost shell s is completely and p is half filled. It is a stable configuration as compared to oxygen whose electronic configuration is 1s2 2s2 2p4. Oxygen will rapidly loose its extra p-electron to acquire a N-like stable configuration.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: How would you explain the fact that the first ionization enthalpy of sodium is lower than that of magnesium but its second ionization enthalpy is higher than that of magnesium?
question_html: How would you explain the fact that the first ionization enthalpy of sodium is lower than that of magnesium but its second ionization enthalpy is higher than that of magnesium?
solution_text: Electronic configurations of Na and Mg are Na = 1s 2 2s 2 2p 6 3s 1 Mg = 1s 2 2s 2 2p 6 3s 2 The 1 st ionization enthalpy of magnesium is higher than that of Na due to higher nuclear charge and slightly smaller atomic radius of Mg than Na. Electronic configurations of Na and Mg after loosing 1 electron are Na + = 1s 2 2s 2 2p 6 Mg + = 1s 2 2s 2 2p 6 3s 1 After the loss of the first electron, Na + formed has the electronic configuration of neon (2,8). The higher stability of the completely filled noble gas configuration leads to very high second ionization enthalpy for sodium. On the other hand, Mg + formed after losing the first electron still has one more electron in its outermost orbital. Therefore, the second ionization enthalpy of magnesium is much smaller than that of sodium.
solution_html:
Electronic configurations of Na and Mg are
Na = 1s22s22p63s1
Mg = 1s22s22p63s2
The 1st ionization enthalpy of magnesium is higher than that of Na due to higher nuclear charge and slightly smaller atomic radius of Mg than Na.
Electronic configurations of Na and Mg after loosing 1 electron are
Na+ = 1s22s22p6
Mg+ = 1s22s22p63s1
After the loss of the first electron, Na+ formed has the electronic configuration of neon (2,8). The higher stability of the completely filled noble gas configuration leads to very high second ionization enthalpy for sodium. On the other hand, Mg+ formed after losing the first electron still has one more electron in its outermost orbital. Therefore, the second ionization enthalpy of magnesium is much smaller than that of sodium.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: What are the various factors due to which the ionization enthalpy of the main group elements tends to decrease down the group?
question_html: What are the various factors due to which the ionization enthalpy of the main group elements tends to decrease down the group?
solution_text: Atomic size. With the increase in atomic size, the number of electron shells increase.Therefore, the force that binds the electrons with the nucleus decreases.Therefore ionization enthalpy decreases with the increase in atomic size. Screening or shielding effect of inner shell electron. With the addition of new shells, the number of inner electron shells which shield the valence electrons increases. As a result, the force of attraction of the nucleus for the valence electrons further decreases so less amount of energy is requied to remove the valence electron and hence the ionization enthalpy decreases.
solution_html:
Atomic size. With the increase in atomic size, the number of electron shells increase.Therefore, the force that binds the electrons with the nucleus decreases.Therefore  ionization enthalpy  decreases with the increase in atomic size.
Screening or shielding effect of inner shell electron. With the addition of new shells, the number of inner electron shells which shield the valence electrons increases. As a result, the force of attraction of the nucleus for the valence electrons further decreases so less amount of energy is requied to remove the valence electron and hence the ionization enthalpy decreases.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: The first ionization enthalpy values (in kJ mol -1 ) of group 13 elements are: B - 801, Al - 577, Ga - 579, In - 558, Tl - 589 How would you explain deviation from the general trend?
question_html:
The first ionization enthalpy values (in kJ mol-1) of group 13 elements are:
B - 801, Al - 577, Ga - 579, In - 558, Tl - 589
How would you explain deviation from the general trend?
solution_text: The decrease in {tex}{\Delta _i}{H_1}{/tex} value from B to Al is due to the bigger size of Al. In Ga there is 10 , 3d electrons which do not screen as is done by S and P electrons. Therefore, there is an unexpected increase in the magnitude of effective nuclear charge resulting in increased {tex}{\Delta _i}{H_1}{/tex} values. The same is with Ti. The later has fourteen {tex}\Delta f{/tex} electrons with very poor shielding effect. This also increases, the effective nuclear charge thus the value of {tex}{\Delta _i}{H_1}{/tex} increases.
solution_html: The decrease in {tex}{\Delta _i}{H_1}{/tex} value from B to Al is due to the bigger size of Al. In Ga there is 10 , 3d electrons which do not screen as is done by S and P electrons. Therefore, there is an unexpected increase in the magnitude of effective nuclear charge resulting in increased {tex}{\Delta _i}{H_1}{/tex} values. The same is with Ti. The later has fourteen {tex}\Delta f{/tex} electrons with very poor shielding effect. This also increases, the effective nuclear charge thus the value of {tex}{\Delta _i}{H_1}{/tex} increases.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Which of the following pairs of elements would have a more negative electron gain enthalpy? O or F F or Cl
question_html:
Which of the following pairs of elements would have a more negative electron gain enthalpy?

  1. O or F
  2. F or Cl
solution_text: Electron gain enthalpy generally becomes more negative across a period as we move from left to right. On moving from oxygen to fluorine, the effective nuclear charge increases and atomic size decreases with increase in atomic number. Due to this, forces of attraction of the nucleus increases for the incoming electron. That’s why electron gain enthalpy becomes more negative for fluorine than that of oxygen. Within a group, electron gain enthalpy becomes less negative down a group. But electron gain enthalpy of chlorine is more negative than that of the fluorine. This is due to the small size of fluorine as the electron-electron repulsions in relatively compact 2p-orbital is greater than that in the larger 3p-orbital and hence, the incoming electron feels greater repulsion in fluorine than in the chlorine.
solution_html:
1. Electron gain enthalpy generally becomes more negative across a period as we move from left to right. On moving from oxygen to fluorine, the effective nuclear charge increases and atomic size decreases with increase in atomic number. Due to this, forces of attraction of the nucleus increases for the incoming electron. That’s why electron gain enthalpy becomes more negative for fluorine than that of oxygen.
  2. Within a group, electron gain enthalpy becomes less negative down a group. But electron gain enthalpy of chlorine is more negative than that of the fluorine. This is due to the small size of fluorine as the electron-electron repulsions in relatively compact 2p-orbital is greater than that in the larger 3p-orbital and hence, the incoming electron feels greater repulsion in fluorine than in the chlorine.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Would you expect the second electron gain enthalpy of O as positive, more negative or less negative than the first? Justify your answer.
question_html: Would you expect the second electron gain enthalpy of O as positive, more negative or less negative than the first? Justify your answer.
solution_text: For oxygen atom: O(g) + e - {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} O - (g) ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} eg H = - 141 kJ mol -1 ) O - (g) + e - {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} O 2- (g) ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} eg H = +780 kJ mol-1) The first electron gain enthalpy of oxygen is negative because energy is released when a gaseous atom accepts an electron to form monovalent anion (O - ). The second electron gain enthalpy is positive because O - ion will repel the incoming electron hence energy is needed by the electron to overcome such a force of repulsion.
solution_html:
For oxygen atom:
O(g) + e- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} O-(g) ({tex}\Delta{/tex}eg H = - 141 kJ mol-1)
O-(g) + e- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} O2-(g) ({tex}\Delta{/tex}eg H = +780 kJ mol-1)
The first electron gain enthalpy of oxygen is negative because energy is released when a gaseous atom accepts an electron to form monovalent anion (O-). The second electron gain enthalpy is positive because O- ion will repel the incoming electron hence energy is needed by the electron to overcome such a force of repulsion.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: What is basic difference between the terms electron gain enthalpy and electro-negativity?
question_html: What is basic difference between the terms electron gain enthalpy and electro-negativity?
solution_text: Electron gain enthalpy refers to tendency of an isolated gaseous atom to accept an additional electron to form a negative ion. It is an experimentally determined quantity with units of kJ/mol. It may be positive or negative depending on the nature of the element. Whereas electro-negativity refers to tendency of the atom of an element to attract shared pair of electrons towards it in a covalent bond. It is not a measurable quantity and so it has no unit. There are different scales for measuring the electronegativity of elements. The most commonly used is the Pauling Electronegativity scale developed by Linus Pauling.
solution_html: Electron gain enthalpy refers to tendency of an isolated gaseous atom to accept an additional electron to form a negative ion. It is an experimentally determined quantity with units of kJ/mol. It may be positive or negative depending on the nature of the element. Whereas electro-negativity refers to tendency of the atom of an element to attract shared pair of electrons towards it in a covalent bond. It is not a measurable quantity and so it has no unit. There are different scales for measuring the electronegativity of elements. The most commonly used is the Pauling Electronegativity scale developed by Linus Pauling.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: How would you react to the statement that the electro-negativity of N on Pauling scale is 3.0 in all the nitrogen compounds?
question_html: How would you react to the statement that the electro-negativity of N on Pauling scale is 3.0 in all the nitrogen compounds?
solution_text: No, it is not correct to say that the electronegativity of nitrogen in all the compounds is 3. It depends upon nitrogens's state of hybridisation in a particular compound. In principle, greater the percentage of s-character, more will be the electronegativity of the element. Thus, the electronegativity of nitrogen increases in moving from sp 3 to sp hybridised orbitals in the following manner, sp 3 < sp 2 < sp.
solution_html: No, it is not correct to say that the electronegativity of nitrogen in all the compounds is 3. It depends upon nitrogens's state of hybridisation in a particular compound. In principle, greater the percentage of s-character, more will be the electronegativity of the element. Thus, the electronegativity of nitrogen increases in moving from sp3  to sp hybridised orbitals in the following manner, sp3 < sp2 < sp.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Describe the theory associated with the radius of an atom as it: gains an electron loses an electron
question_html:
Describe the theory associated with the radius of an atom as it:

  1. gains an electron
  2. loses an electron
solution_text: When an atom gains an electron, it forms an anion. The size of an anion is larger than that of the parent atom because the addition of one or more electrons results in increased repulsion among electrons and a decrease in effective nuclear charge. For example the ionic radius of fluoride ion (F - ) is 136 pm whereas the atomic radius of Fluorine (F) is only 64 pm. When an atom loses an electron, it forms a cation. A cation is smaller than its parent atom because it has lesser electrons while its nuclear charge remains the same. This implies that the valence electrons are more tightly held towards the nucleus thereby reducing the size. For example, the atomic radius of sodium (Na) is 186 pm and atomic radius of sodium ion (Na + ) = 95 pm.
solution_html:
1. When an atom gains an electron, it forms an anion. The size of an anion is larger than that of the parent atom because the addition of one or more electrons results in increased repulsion among electrons and a decrease in effective nuclear charge. For example the ionic radius of fluoride ion (F-) is 136 pm whereas the atomic radius of Fluorine (F) is only 64 pm.
  2. When an atom loses an electron, it forms a cation. A cation is smaller than its parent atom because it has lesser electrons while its nuclear charge remains the same. This implies that the valence electrons are more tightly held towards the nucleus thereby reducing the size. For example, the atomic radius of sodium (Na) is 186 pm and atomic radius of sodium ion (Na+) = 95 pm.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Would you expect the first ionization enthalpies of two isotopes of the same element to be the same or different?
question_html: Would you expect the first ionization enthalpies of two isotopes of the same element to be the same or different?
solution_text: Isotopes are elements with the same atomic number but different mass numbers. It implies that all isotopes of the same atom have the same electronic configurations; hence the same ionization enthalpies.
solution_html: Isotopes are elements with the same atomic number but different mass numbers. It implies that all isotopes of the same atom have the same electronic configurations; hence the same ionization enthalpies.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: What are major differences between metals and non-metals?
question_html: What are major differences between metals and non-metals?
solution_text: The major difference between metals and non-metals is their ionization eneregies. Metals have low ionization energies and due to this they have a strong tendency to lose electrons to form cations. Also, metals are strong reducing agents and form basic oxides and ionic compounds. Non-metals prefer to accept electrons to form anions i.e. they have high electron affinity. Moreover, non-metals are strong oxidizing agent and form acidic oxides and covalent compounds.
solution_html: The major difference between metals and non-metals is their ionization eneregies. Metals have low ionization energies and due to this they have a strong tendency to lose electrons to form cations. Also, metals are strong reducing agents and form basic oxides and ionic compounds. Non-metals prefer to accept electrons to form anions i.e. they have high electron affinity. Moreover, non-metals are strong oxidizing agent and form acidic oxides and covalent compounds.

solution_html: Group 17 has metal, non–metal, liquid as well as gas at room temperature.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: The increasing order of reactivity among group 1 elements is Li < Na < k < Rb < Cs whereas that of group 17 is F > Cl > Br > I. Explain?
question_html: The increasing order of reactivity among group 1 elements is Li < Na < k < Rb < Cs whereas that of group 17 is F > Cl > Br > I. Explain?
solution_text: The elements of Group I have only one electron in their respective valence shells and thus have a strong tendency to lose this electron. The tendency to lose electrons in turn, depends upon the ionization enthalpy. Since the ionization enthalpy decreases down the group therefore, the reactivity of group 1 elements increases in the same order Li < Na < K < Rb < Cs. In contrast, the elements of group 17 have seven electrons in their respective valence shells and thus have strong tendency to accept one more electron to make stable configuration. It is linked with electron gain enthalpy and electro-negativity.As electro-negativity and electron gain enthalpy decreases from F - Cl , the reactivity therefore decreases.
solution_html: The elements of Group I have only one electron in their respective valence shells and thus have a strong tendency to lose this electron. The tendency to lose electrons in turn, depends upon the ionization enthalpy. Since the ionization enthalpy decreases down the group therefore, the reactivity of group 1 elements increases in the same order Li < Na < K < Rb < Cs. In contrast, the elements of group 17 have seven electrons in their respective valence shells and thus have strong tendency to accept one more electron to make stable configuration. It is linked with electron gain enthalpy and electro-negativity.As electro-negativity and electron gain enthalpy decreases from F - Cl , the reactivity therefore decreases.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Write the general outer electronic configuration of s, p ,d and f-block elements?
question_html: Write the general outer electronic configuration of s, p ,d and f-block elements?
solution_text: The general outer electronic configuration are: (i) s-Block elements : ns 1-2 where n = 2 - 7 (ii) p-Block elements : ns 2 np 1-6 where n = 2 - 6 (iii) d-Block elements : (n-1)d 1-10 ns 0-2 where n = 4 - 7 (iv) f-Block elements : (n-2)f 0-14 (n-1)d 0-1 ns 2 where n = 6 - 7
solution_html:
The general outer electronic configuration are:

(i) s-Block elements : ns1-2  where n = 2 - 7
(ii) p-Block elements : ns2 np1-6 where n = 2 - 6
(iii) d-Block elements : (n-1)d1-10 ns0-2 where n = 4 - 7
(iv) f-Block elements : (n-2)f0-14 (n-1)d0-1 ns2 where n = 6 - 7

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Assign the position of the element having outer electronic configuration in the periodic table. ns 2 np 4 for n = 3 (n - 1)d 2 ns 2 for n = 4 and (n - 2)f 7 (n - 1)d 1 ns 2 for n = 6
question_html:
Assign the position of the element having outer electronic configuration in the periodic table.

  1. ns2 np4 for n = 3
  2. (n - 1)d2 ns2 for n = 4 and
  3. (n - 2)f7 (n - 1)d1 ns2 for n = 6
solution_text: n = 3 Thus elements belong to 3 rd period, belong to p-block element. Since the valence shell contains = 6 electrons Group No. = 10 + 6 = 16 Configuration = 1s 2 2s 2 2p 6 3s 2 3p 4 The element name is sulphur. n = 4 Means element belongs to 4 th period and 4 th group as in the valence shell (2 + 2 ) = 4 electrons. Electronic configuration.= 1s 2 2s 2 2p 6 3s 2 3p 6 3d 2 4s 2 the element name is Titanium. n = 6 Means the element belongs to 6 th period. The last electron goes to the f-orbital, the element is from f-block. Group = 3 The element is gadolinium (z = 64) Complete electronic configuration = [X e ] 4f 7 5d 1 6s 2
solution_html:
1. n = 3
    Thus elements belong to 3 rd period, belong to p-block element.
    Since the valence shell contains = 6 electrons
    Group No. = 10 + 6 = 16
    Configuration = 1s2 2s2 2p6 3s2 3p4
    The element name is sulphur.
  2. n = 4
    Means element belongs to 4 th period and 4th group as in the valence shell (2 + 2 ) = 4 electrons.
    Electronic configuration.= 1s2 2s2 2p6 3s2 3p6 3d2 4s2
         the element name is Titanium.
  3. n = 6
    Means the element belongs to 6 th period.
    The last electron goes to the f-orbital, the element is from f-block.
    Group = 3
    The element is gadolinium (z = 64)
    Complete electronic configuration = [Xe] 4f7 5d1 6s2

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: The first ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} i H 1 ) and the second ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} i H 2 ) ionization enthalpies (in kJ mol –1 ) and the ( {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{eq}} \mathrm{H}{/tex} ) electron gain enthalpy (in kJ mol –1 ) of a few elements are given below: Elements {tex}\Delta H_{1}{/tex} {tex}\Delta H_{2}{/tex} {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{eq}} \mathrm{H}{/tex} I 520 7300 -60 II 419 3051 -48 III 1681 3374 -328 IV 1008 1846 -295 V 2372 5251 +48 VI 738 1451 -40 Which of the above elements is likely to be: the least reactive element. the most reactive metal. the most reactive non-metal. the least reactive non-metal. the metal which can form a stable binary halide of the formula MX 2 (X = halogen). the metal which can form a predominantly stable covalent halide of the formula MX (X = halogen)?
question_html:
The first ({tex}\Delta{/tex}iH1) and the second ({tex}\Delta{/tex}iH2) ionization enthalpies (in kJ mol–1) and the ({tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{eq}} \mathrm{H}{/tex}) electron gain enthalpy (in kJ mol–1) of a few elements are given below:

Elements {tex}\Delta H_{1}{/tex} {tex}\Delta H_{2}{/tex} {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{eq}} \mathrm{H}{/tex}
I 520 7300 -60
II 419 3051 -48
III 1681 3374 -328
IV 1008 1846 -295
V 2372 5251 +48
VI 738 1451 -40

Which of the above elements is likely to be:

  1. the least reactive element.
  2. the most reactive metal.
  3. the most reactive non-metal.
  4. the least reactive non-metal.
  5. the metal which can form a stable binary halide of the formula MX2 (X = halogen).
  6. the metal which can form a predominantly stable covalent halide of the formula MX (X = halogen)?
solution_text: Element (V) is likely to be the least reactive element. This is because it has the highest first ionization enthalpy ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} H 1 ) i.e, 2372 and a positive electron gain enthalpy ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} eg H) i.e, +48. Element (II) is likely to be the most reactive metal as it has the lowest first ionization enthalpy ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} H 1 ) i.e, 419 and a low negative electron gain enthalpy ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} eg H) i.e, -48. Element (III) is likely to be the most reactive non–metal as it has a high first ionization enthalpy ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} H 1 ) i.e,1681 and the highest negative electron gain enthalpy ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} eg H) i.e, -328. Element (V) is likely to be the least reactive non–metal since it has a very high first ionization enthalpy ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} H 1 ) i.e, 2372 and a positive electron gain enthalpy ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} eg H) i.e, +48. Element (VI) has a low negative electron gain enthalpy ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} eg H). Thus, it is metal. Further, it has the lowest second ionization enthalpy ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} H 2 ) i.e, 1451. Hence, it can form a stable binary halide of the formula MX 2 (X = halogen). The element (I) have low first ionization energy i.e, 520 and high second ionization energy i.e, 7300. Therefore, it can form a predominantly stable covalent halide of the formula MX (X = halogen).
solution_html:
1. Element (V) is likely to be the least reactive element. This is because it has the highest first ionization enthalpy ({tex}\Delta{/tex}H1) i.e, 2372 and a positive electron gain enthalpy ({tex}\Delta{/tex}egH) i.e, +48.
  2. Element (II) is likely to be the most reactive metal as it has the lowest first ionization enthalpy ({tex}\Delta{/tex}H1) i.e, 419 and a low negative electron gain enthalpy ({tex}\Delta{/tex}egH) i.e, -48.
  3. Element (III) is likely to be the most reactive non–metal as it has a high first ionization enthalpy ({tex}\Delta{/tex}H1) i.e,1681 and the highest negative electron gain enthalpy ({tex}\Delta{/tex}egH) i.e, -328.
  4. Element (V) is likely to be the least reactive non–metal since it has a very high first ionization enthalpy ({tex}\Delta{/tex}H1) i.e, 2372 and a positive electron gain enthalpy ({tex}\Delta{/tex}egH) i.e, +48.
  5. Element (VI) has a low negative electron gain enthalpy ({tex}\Delta{/tex}egH). Thus, it is metal. Further, it has the lowest second ionization enthalpy ({tex}\Delta{/tex}H2) i.e, 1451. Hence, it can form a stable binary halide of the formula MX2(X = halogen).
  6. The element (I) have low first ionization energy i.e, 520 and high second ionization energy i.e, 7300. Therefore, it can form a predominantly stable covalent halide of the formula MX (X = halogen).

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Predict the formula of the stable binary compounds that would be formed by the combination of the following pairs of elements: Lithium and oxygen Magnesium and nitrogen Aluminium and iodine Silicon and oxygen Phosphorous and fluorine Element 71 and fluorine
question_html:
Predict the formula of the stable binary compounds that would be formed by the combination of the following pairs of elements:

  1. Lithium and oxygen
  2. Magnesium and nitrogen
  3. Aluminium and iodine
  4. Silicon and oxygen
  5. Phosphorous and fluorine
  6. Element 71 and fluorine
solution_text: Alkali metal lithium (with 1 valence electron) combine with group 16 element oxygen (having a valence of 2) to form Li 2 O (Lithium oxide). Alkaline earth metal magnesium (with 2 valence electrons) combines with group 5 element nitrogen (having valence 3) to form Mg 3 N 2 (Magnesium nitride). Group 13 element aluminum (with 3 valence electrons) combines with group 17 element iodine (a halogen with valence 1) to form AlI 3 (Aluminium iodide). Group 14 element silicon (with a valence of 4) combines with group 16 element oxygen ( having valence 2) to form SiO 2 (Silicon dioxide). Group 15 element phosphorous (with valence 3 or 5) combines with group 17 element fluorine (a halogen with valence 1) to form PF 5 (Phosphorous pentafluoride). For Z = 71, the compound formed will be LuF 3 {the element is Lutenium (Lu) with electronic configuration = [Xe] 4f 14 5d 1 6s 2 }.
solution_html:
1. Alkali metal lithium (with 1 valence electron) combine with group 16 element oxygen (having a valence of 2) to form Li2O (Lithium oxide).
  2. Alkaline earth metal magnesium (with 2 valence electrons) combines with group 5 element nitrogen (having valence 3) to form Mg3N2 (Magnesium nitride).
  3. Group 13 element aluminum (with 3 valence electrons) combines with group 17 element iodine (a halogen with valence 1) to form AlI3 (Aluminium iodide).
  4. Group 14 element silicon (with a valence of 4) combines with group 16 element oxygen ( having valence 2) to form SiO2 (Silicon dioxide).
  5. Group 15 element phosphorous (with valence 3 or 5) combines with group 17 element fluorine (a halogen with valence 1) to form PF5 (Phosphorous pentafluoride).
  6. For Z = 71, the compound formed will be LuF3  {the element is Lutenium (Lu) with electronic configuration = [Xe] 4f14 5d1 6s2}.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: In the modern periodic table, the period indicates the value of:
question_html: In the modern periodic table, the period indicates the value of:

options.explanation: In the modern periodic table, each period indicates the value of principal quantum number (n). It also implies the number of shells or orbits.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Which of the following statements related to the modern periodic table is incorrect? The p-block has 6 columns, because a maximum of 6 electrons can occupy all the orbitals in a p-shell. The d-block has 8 columns, because a maximum of 8 electrons can occupy all the orbitals in a d-subshell. Each block contains a number of columns equal to the number of electrons that can occupy that subshell. The block indicates value of azimuthal quantum number (l) for the last subshell that received electrons in building up the electronic configuration.
question_html:
Which of the following statements related to the modern periodic table is incorrect?

  1. The p-block has 6 columns, because a maximum of 6 electrons can occupy all the orbitals in a p-shell.
  2. The d-block has 8 columns, because a maximum of 8 electrons can occupy all the orbitals in a d-subshell.
  3. Each block contains a number of columns equal to the number of electrons that can occupy that subshell.
  4. The block indicates value of azimuthal quantum number (l) for the last subshell that received electrons in building up the electronic configuration.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Anything that influences the valence electrons will affect the chemistry of the element. Which one of the following factors does not affect the valence shell?
question_html: Anything that influences the valence electrons will affect the chemistry of the element. Which one of the following factors does not affect the valence shell?

options.explanation: Nuclear mass or the mass of an atomic nucleus has no role in determining electronic configuration.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: The size of isoelectronic species: F – , Ne and Na + is affected by
question_html: The size of isoelectronic species: F–, Ne and Na+ is affected by

options.explanation: The size of an isoelectronic species increases with a decrease in the nuclear charge (Z). For example, the order of the increasing nuclear charge of F - , Ne, and Na+ is as follows: F - (Z = 9) < Ne(Z = 10) < Na + (Z = 11) Therefore, the order of the increasing size of F - , Ne and Na + is as follows: Na + < Ne < F - Hence, the correct option is - nuclear charge (Z).

options.text: electron-electron interaction in the outer orbitals
none of the factors because their size is the same.
options.html: none of the factors because their size is the same.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Which one of the following statements is incorrect in relation to ionization enthalpy?
question_html: Which one of the following statements is incorrect in relation to ionization enthalpy?

options.text: Ionization enthalpy increases for each successive electron.
options.html: Ionization enthalpy increases for each successive electron.

options.text: The greatest increase in ionization enthalpy is experienced on the removal of an electron from core noble gas configuration.
options.html: The greatest increase in ionization enthalpy is experienced on the removal of an electron from core noble gas configuration.

options.text: The end of valence electrons is marked by a big jump in ionization enthalpy.
options.html: The end of valence electrons is marked by a big jump in ionization enthalpy.

options.text: Removal of an electron from orbitals bearing lower n value is easier than from orbital having higher n value.
options.html: Removal of an electron from orbitals bearing lower n value is easier than from orbital having higher n value.
options.explanation: Electrons in orbitals bearing a lower n value are more attracted to the nucleus than electrons in orbitals bearing a higher n value. Hence, the removal of electrons from orbitals bearing a higher n value is easier than the removal of electrons from orbitals having a lower n value. Hence, the incorrect statement in relation to ionization enthalpy is the removal of an electron from orbitals bearing lower n value is easier than from orbital having higher n value.

correct_option_text: Removal of an electron from orbitals bearing lower n value is easier than from orbital having higher n value.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Considering the elements B, Al, Mg and K, the correct order of their metallic character is:
question_html: Considering the elements B, Al, Mg and K, the correct order of their metallic character is:

options.explanation: The metallic character depends on the ease with which an atom can loose electron i.e. its the ionisation energy. Lower the ionization energy higher is the metallic character. The ionization energy is of the order K < Mg < Al < B; hence the metallic character is reverse of that K > Mg > Al > B.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Considering the elements B, C, N, F and Si, the correct order of their non-metallic character is:
question_html: Considering the elements B, C, N, F and Si, the correct order of their non-metallic character is:

options.explanation: In a period, the non-metallic character increases from left to right. Thus, among B, C, N and F, non-metallic character decreases in the order: F > N > C > B. However, within a group, non-metallic character decreases from top to bottom. Thus, C is more non-metallic than Si. Therefore, the correct sequence of decreasing non-metallic character is: F > N > C > B > Si. The same can be verified with first ionization potential of the atoms. The values are F (17.42) > N (14.53) > C (11.26) > B (8.298) > Si (8.151). Lower ionization potential implies easy tendency to loose electrons. The potential of B and Si appears to be close but it is the bigger size of Si that helps it loose electron comparatively faster.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
question_text: Considering the elements F, Cl, O and N, the correct order of their chemical reactivity in terms of oxidizing property is:
question_html: Considering the elements F, Cl, O and N, the correct order of their chemical reactivity in terms of oxidizing property is:

options.explanation: The oxidising character of elements increases with increase in atomic number from left to right across a period. Thus, we get the decreasing order of oxidizing property as F > O > N. Again, the oxidising character of elements decreases down a group. Thus, we get F > Cl. However, the oxidizing character of O is more than that of Cl i.e., O > Cl. Hence, the correct order of chemical reactivity of F, Cl, O, and N in terms of their oxidizing property is F > O > Cl > N.
